pairs.question: What was the primary motivation behind creating the system design canvas app?
pairs.answer: The creator was frustrated with bloated web applications for practicing system design problems or creating diagrams, so they decided to build their own lightweight and customizable solution.

pairs.question: What were the five main steps taken to develop the initial version of the app?
pairs.answer: 1) Added arrows to connect components, 2) Implemented drag-and-drop editing for components (moving, resizing, deleting), 3) Made the canvas size infinite, 4) Added zoom functionality via mouse scroll, and 5) Expanded the component library and added a scrollbar for the components section.

pairs.question: How does the export functionality work in the system design canvas app?
pairs.answer: The app includes an export feature that allows users to save their designs as PNG or JPEG files by selecting the desired format from the available options.

pairs.question: What are some of the future features planned for the system design canvas app?
pairs.answer: Future enhancements include adding flow chart creation options, enabling single-element copying, implementing multi-element movement, and expanding the component library further.

pairs.question: How did the creator interact with Google AI Studio during development?
pairs.answer: The creator used Google AI Studio (then called 'Gemini') for 'vibe coding,' but occasionally had to explicitly ask it to continue making code changes when it would pause midway through implementation.

pairs.question: What technical challenges did the creator face while developing the app?
pairs.answer: The main technical challenge was ensuring smooth drag-and-drop interactions and infinite canvas scaling without performance degradation, which required careful state management and event handling.
